gold_final_answer: All functions f(x) = ax + b with a ≠ 0.
topics_flat: Algebra > Algebraic Expressions > Functional Equations > Injectivity / surjectivity
response_text:
Let us write property (ii) in the equivalent form:

If  
\[
2f(b)=f(a)+f(c),
\]
then  
\[
2b=a+c.
\]

We will show that the only continuous surjective functions satisfying (i) and (ii) are affine functions
\[
f(x)=mx+n
\]
with \(m\neq 0\), and then use (i) to determine the restriction on \(m,n\).

---

## 1) \(f\) is injective

Assume \(f(u)=f(v)\). Then \(f(u),f(v),f(v)\) are in arithmetic progression, so by (ii), \(u,v,v\) are in arithmetic progression. Hence
\[
2v=u+v \implies u=v.
\]
So \(f\) is injective.

Since \(f\) is continuous and surjective, it is a continuous bijection \(\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\), hence a homeomorphism.

---

## 2) The midpoint property

Take any \(x,y\in\mathbb R\), and let
\[
z=\frac{x+y}{2}.
\]
Then \(x,z,y\) are in arithmetic progression, so by (ii), \(f(x),f(z),f(y)\) are in arithmetic progression. Therefore
\[
2f\!\left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)=f(x)+f(y). \tag{1}
\]

This is Jensen’s functional equation.

---

## 3) Continuity implies affinity

A continuous solution of Jensen’s equation on \(\mathbb R\) is affine:
\[
f(x)=mx+n
\]
for some constants \(m,n\). Since \(f\) is surjective, \(m\neq 0\).

---

## 4) Use condition (i)

Substitute \(f(x)=mx+n\) into
\[
2f(1)=f(0)+f(2):
\]
\[
2(m+n)=n+(2m+n).
\]
This is an identity, so condition (i) imposes no further restriction.

Thus every nonconstant affine function works.

---

## 5) Verification

If \(f(x)=mx+n\) with \(m\neq 0\), then
\[
f(a),f(b),f(c)\text{ in AP}
\iff 2f(b)=f(a)+f(c)
\iff 2mb+2n=ma+mc+2n
\iff 2b=a+c,
\]
so (ii) holds. Also (i) holds automatically.

Therefore the complete set of functions is:

\[
f(x)=mx+n \qquad (m\neq 0).
\]

Final answer: \(\boxed{f(x)=mx+n\text{ for arbitrary }n\in\mathbb R,\ m\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}}\).
